title: Zedong 254
author: Zedong 254
content:
Mao Zedong (1893–1976) was a Chinese revolutionary and founding father of the People’s Republic of China (PRC), which he ruled from 1949 until his death. Born in Hunan province, Mao became involved in Marxist and nationalist movements during his youth and played a central role in the formation of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) in 1921. He led the Red Army in the Chinese Civil War, ultimately defeating the Nationalist forces of Chiang Kai-shek.

As China’s leader, Mao implemented radical socio-economic reforms, including land redistribution and the collectivization of agriculture. His ambitious “Great Leap Forward” (1958–1962) aimed to rapidly industrialize China but led to a catastrophic famine that caused millions of deaths. Later, he launched the “Cultural Revolution” (1966–1976) to preserve communist ideology by purging perceived capitalist and traditional elements, which resulted in widespread chaos, persecution, and cultural destruction.

Despite the turmoil, Mao is credited with unifying China, expanding literacy, and asserting its independence on the world stage. His leadership remains controversial—revered by some as a symbol of national pride and revolution, and criticized by others for the immense human suffering caused by his policies. Mao’s influence continues to shape modern China’s politics and ideology.
